text: The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and makes an incision just inferior to it. The view improves as the assistant pulls up the transverse colon, enhancing visualization of the root of the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon dissects posteriorly, identifying the duodenum, and performs a medial to lateral dissection, revealing the ureter.
The surgeon cleans the ileocolic pedicle and prepares to ligate it using the vessel sealer extend.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, extending cephalad towards the gallbladder and clearing tissue off the duodenum. The transverse colon is dropped down to mobilize the hepatic flexure, revealing adhesions to the gallbladder. The gallbladder and liver are retracted cephalad to expose the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon navigates the anatomical structures, distinguishing between the duodenum and transverse colon, which initially appeared to extend under the gallbladder. The separation between these structures becomes visible, and the surgeon completes the lateral dissection, meeting the medial to lateral dissection plane. Scissors are used for further dissection.

The surgeon retracts the gallbladder inferiorly and medially using the fourth arm, completing the medial to lateral dissection. The appendix becomes visible as the terminal ileum is elevated, and the surgeon finishes mobilizing the right colon by freeing the lateral attachments.

The surgeon cleans off the proximal and distal mesentery, focusing on the terminal ileum. The proximal transverse colon is visible, and the right branch of the middle colic artery is identified and cauterized twice before being transected.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to check the perfusion of the tissue. The specimen is placed aside after being fully mobilized, and attention is turned towards performing the anastomosis.

The surgeon aligns the bowel for a side-to-side isoparastaltic anastomosis using a 60mm stapler with a white load, while a blue load is used to transect the bowel. The stapler is inserted through the suprapubic port, which will be extended for the extraction site. The surgeon carefully removes tension from the retraction suture to prevent tissue displacement during stapling.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to close the common channel, employing a technique of stitching from in to out and out to in. A mistake occurs when the stitch is pulled against the grain, causing oozing. The surgeon then transitions to a canal stitch to resolve the issue.
The surgeon runs the suture along the bowel, imbricating the tissue, and then performs a reinforcement stitch using silk to suspend the colon.
